questions.question: A business purchases goods on credit from a supplier. Which of the following correctly describes the effect on the accounting equation?
options: Assets decrease and liabilities decrease by the same amount
Assets only increase with no change to liabilities
Assets increase (stock) and liabilities increase (creditor) by the same amount
Capital decreases and assets decrease by the same amount
questions.explanation: Buying on credit increases Stock/Inventory (an asset) and increases Creditors (a liability) by the same amount. The equation Assets = Liabilities + Capital remains balanced.

questions.question: A debtor is a person or business that the company owes money to.
questions.explanation: A debtor owes money TO the business (e.g., a customer who bought on credit). A creditor is someone the business owes money to (e.g., a supplier). This is a very common mix-up.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a source document used in bookkeeping?
options: Sales invoice
Bank statement
Trial balance
Credit note
questions.explanation: A trial balance is an internal accounting report, not a source document. Source documents are the original records of transactions: invoices, bank statements, receipts, credit notes — these originate from external parties or payment systems.

questions.question: Capital is classified on the credit side of the accounting equation because, from the business's perspective, it is money owed back to the owner.
questions.explanation: Applying the entity concept, the business is separate from its owner. Capital contributed by the owner is money the business owes back to the owner, so it sits on the liability (credit) side: Assets = Liabilities + Capital.

questions.question: Which of the following are examples of current assets? Select all that apply.
options: Bank balance
Trade debtors
Motor vehicles
Prepaid expenses
questions.explanation: Bank balances, trade debtors, and prepaid expenses are current assets (expected to convert to cash within 12 months). Motor vehicles are non-current (fixed) assets — they are long-term and not quickly converted to cash.

questions.question: Murphy's Bakery has total assets of €45,000 and total liabilities of €18,000. What is the owner's capital?
questions.explanation: Capital = Assets − Liabilities = €45,000 − €18,000 = €27,000. Rearranging the fundamental equation: Assets = Liabilities + Capital → Capital = Assets − Liabilities.

questions.question: A business has total assets of €32,500 and owner's capital of €19,800. What are its total liabilities? Enter the amount in euros.
questions.explanation: Liabilities = Assets − Capital = €32,500 − €19,800 = €12,700. This rearrangement of Assets = Liabilities + Capital is one of the most common exam calculations.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key advantage of a computerised accounting system over a manual system?
options: Lower initial setup cost
Automatic double-entry posting from a single transaction entry
No risk of data loss
Less need for trained staff
questions.explanation: In computerised systems, entering one transaction creates both sides of the double-entry automatically. Manual systems require the bookkeeper to post each entry individually — slower and more error-prone.

questions.question: In a computerised accounting system, a full backup is only needed at the end of the financial year.
questions.explanation: Backups should be taken regularly — ideally daily or after every processing session. Hardware failure, ransomware, or accidental deletion can occur at any time. Waiting until year-end could mean losing months of data.

questions.question: Sage 50 and QuickBooks are examples of:
options: Spreadsheet software
Payroll-only software
Double-entry computerised accounting packages
Tax-filing software
questions.explanation: Sage 50 and QuickBooks are full double-entry bookkeeping/accounting software packages used by businesses to process transactions, maintain ledgers, generate reports, and produce financial statements.

questions.question: Which of the following are disadvantages of a manual bookkeeping system? Select all that apply.
options: Time-consuming to post each entry individually
Higher risk of arithmetic errors
Cannot automatically generate VAT or aged debtor reports
Always cheaper than computerised systems in the long run
questions.explanation: Manual systems are slow (A), prone to arithmetic error (B), and can't auto-generate reports (C). The claim that manual is always cheaper (D) is false — staff time is costly, and accounting software pays for itself quickly through efficiency gains.

questions.question: An audit trail in a computerised accounting system records every transaction change, showing who made it and when.
questions.explanation: An audit trail is a key security and governance feature of computerised systems. It logs every entry, modification, and deletion with a timestamp and user ID — far easier to review than a manual ledger.

questions.question: A business is setting up Sage 50 for the first time. What is the very first step before entering any transactions?
options: Enter all current-year transactions
Set up the chart of accounts and opening balances
Generate a trial balance
Create a full backup
questions.explanation: The chart of accounts (the structured list of all ledger codes and names) must be configured and opening balances entered before any transactions can be correctly categorised or reported.

questions.question: What does 'real-time processing' mean in a computerised accounting system?
options: Transactions are only processed at night in batches
Transactions are processed and accounts updated immediately as they are entered
The system processes only bank-related transactions instantly
Reports are generated automatically at month-end
questions.explanation: Real-time processing means that as soon as a transaction is entered, all related accounts update immediately. This contrasts with batch processing, where transactions are collected and processed together at a scheduled time.

questions.question: Murphy's Bakery sells goods on credit to City Café — Invoice 202 for €738 (€600 net + 23% VAT). In which book of first entry is this recorded?
options: Cash Book (receipts side)
Sales Day Book
General Journal
Petty Cash Book
questions.explanation: Credit sales are recorded in the Sales Day Book. The Cash Book only records cash/bank transactions. City Café hasn't paid yet — this is a credit transaction, so it goes in the Sales Day Book.

questions.question: Cash sales and credit sales are both recorded in the Sales Day Book.
questions.explanation: Only CREDIT sales go in the Sales Day Book. Cash sales (where payment is received immediately) are recorded in the Cash Book on the receipts (debit) side — not in the Sales Day Book.

questions.question: A business issues credit note CN01 to Hotel Plaza for €123 (€100 net + 23% VAT) for returned goods. Where is this recorded?
options: Purchases Returns Day Book
Sales Day Book (as a negative entry)
Sales Returns Day Book
General Journal
questions.explanation: Credit notes issued TO customers (for returned goods or overcharges) go in the Sales Returns Day Book. The Purchases Returns Day Book is used when the business itself returns goods to a supplier.

questions.question: Which of the following transactions would appear in the Cash Book? Select all that apply.
options: Payment of wages by bank transfer
Credit purchase from a supplier
Cash sales lodged directly to the bank
Receipt from a debtor by cheque
questions.explanation: The Cash Book records all bank and cash movements. Wages by bank transfer, cash sales lodged, and cheque receipts from debtors all move money through the bank. A credit purchase doesn't involve cash — it goes in the Purchases Day Book.

questions.question: Under the imprest system of petty cash, the petty cash float is topped up at regular intervals to restore it to the same fixed amount.
questions.explanation: The imprest system works like this: a fixed float (e.g., €200) is established. At any time, receipts + remaining cash = the original float. At period-end, the float is reimbursed back to €200. This provides a simple, verifiable control mechanism.

questions.question: Murphy's Bakery's Sales Day Book for March shows: Invoice 201 — Hotel Plaza €984 gross; Invoice 202 — City Café €738 gross. VAT is 23%. What is the total NET sales figure?
The General Journal is primarily used to record:
options: All credit sales transactions
Transactions not covered by the specialised day books (e.g., corrections, depreciation, opening entries)
All bank transactions
All payroll transactions
questions.explanation: The General Journal (Journal Proper) handles transactions that don't fit the specialised books — corrections of errors, opening entries, depreciation, asset disposals, and other non-routine items.

questions.question: Murphy's Bakery pays wages of €900 by cheque (Chq 101). The correct double-entry posting is:
options: Debit Bank Account / Credit Wages Account
Debit Wages Account / Credit Bank Account
Debit Wages Account / Credit Capital Account
Debit Sales Account / Credit Wages Account
questions.explanation: Wages is an expense — expenses increase with a Debit. The bank balance decreases — assets decrease with a Credit. So: DR Wages Account / CR Bank Account (Cash Book).

questions.question: In double-entry bookkeeping, every transaction is recorded as a debit in one account and a credit in another, with total debits always equalling total credits.
questions.explanation: This is the fundamental rule of double-entry bookkeeping. For every transaction, the debit amount equals the credit amount. The Trial Balance tests this equality across all accounts.

questions.question: Hotel Plaza pays their outstanding debt of €1,230 by bank transfer. How is this posted?
options: DR Hotel Plaza Account / CR Bank Account
DR Bank Account / CR Hotel Plaza Account
DR Sales Account / CR Bank Account
DR Bank Account / CR Sales Account
questions.explanation: When a debtor pays: the Bank (asset) increases → Debit Bank. The debtor's balance reduces (they owe less) → Credit Hotel Plaza's account. Money comes IN to the bank, and the debtor's debt goes OUT.

questions.question: Which of the following accounts have a normal CREDIT balance? Select all that apply.
options: Capital Account
Sales Account
Creditors Account
Purchases Account
questions.explanation: Capital, Sales, and Creditors all increase on the credit side — they have normal credit balances. Purchases is an expense account that increases with debits, so it has a normal debit balance.

questions.question: Flour Mills Ltd supplies goods on credit — Invoice FM23: €500 net + 23% VAT. What gross amount is credited to the Creditors — Flour Mills Ltd ledger account?
questions.explanation: Gross = Net × 1.23 = €500 × 1.23 = €615. The creditor's account is credited with the full gross (€615) because that is the total owed. The VAT portion (€115) is separately debited to VAT Control as Input VAT.

questions.question: The Sales Ledger contains the personal accounts of all suppliers to whom the business owes money.
questions.explanation: The Sales Ledger (Debtors Ledger) contains accounts for CUSTOMERS who owe money to the business. Supplier accounts are in the Purchases Ledger (Creditors Ledger). This is a very common mix-up in exams.

questions.question: Hotel Plaza's account: Opening debit balance €1,230; Invoice 201 adds €984 Dr; Credit Note CN01 reduces by €123 Cr; Payment received €1,230 Cr. What is the closing balance?
questions.explanation: Opening €1,230 Dr + Invoice €984 Dr − Credit Note €123 Cr − Payment €1,230 Cr = (1,230 + 984) − (123 + 1,230) = 2,214 − 1,353 = €861 Dr. Hotel Plaza still owes €861.

questions.question: What does a balanced Trial Balance actually prove?
options: There are no errors in the accounts
All transactions have been recorded
Total debits equal total credits
The financial statements are accurate
questions.explanation: A Trial Balance only confirms that debit totals = credit totals. It does NOT prove error-free accounts — errors of omission, commission, principle, compensating errors, and complete reversals all leave the TB balanced.

questions.question: A compensating error occurs when two equal and opposite errors cancel each other out, leaving the Trial Balance still balanced despite both errors existing.
questions.explanation: Compensating errors are hidden because they neutralise each other — e.g., overstating debits by €500 in one account and overstating credits by €500 in another. The TB balances but two mistakes are present.

questions.question: Which type of error would NOT be revealed by an out-of-balance Trial Balance?
options: Posting a debit entry without a corresponding credit
Recording a purchase of equipment in the Repairs expense account (error of principle)
Adding a column incorrectly on one side only
Posting €930 as €390 on the debit side only (transposition)
questions.explanation: An error of principle posts to the wrong ACCOUNT TYPE (e.g., asset treated as expense), but still records equal debits and credits — so the TB stays balanced. Options A, C, and D all create an imbalance because one side is wrong without a corresponding error on the other.

questions.question: Which errors WOULD cause the Trial Balance to fail to balance? Select all that apply.
options: Transposition error on one side only (e.g., €390 instead of €930)
Complete omission of a transaction from the books
A debit entry posted without any corresponding credit
Error of principle (correct amounts, wrong account type)
questions.explanation: A one-sided transposition (A) and a single-sided posting (C) break the debit = credit balance. Complete omission (B) misses both sides equally, so the TB still balances. An error of principle (D) has equal debits and credits — just in the wrong accounts.

questions.question: Murphy's Bakery's Trial Balance has total debit balances of €15,314. What must the total credit balances equal?
questions.explanation: In a correctly prepared Trial Balance, total debits MUST equal total credits. If debits = €15,314 then credits = €15,314. Any difference signals an error in the books.

questions.question: The Trial Balance includes both balance sheet accounts (assets, liabilities, capital) AND income statement accounts (sales, purchases, expenses).
questions.explanation: The Trial Balance extracts every ledger account balance — permanent accounts (assets, liabilities, capital) and temporary accounts (income, expenses). It is a complete snapshot of all account balances before preparing final accounts.

questions.question: Murphy's Bakery's Bank account shows a debit balance of €3,813 in the Trial Balance. This means:
options: The business owes the bank €3,813 (overdraft)
The business has €3,813 in the bank
Total bank payments were €3,813
Total bank receipts were €3,813
questions.explanation: A debit balance in the Bank account means the business holds a positive balance of €3,813. An overdraft (the bank is owed money) would show as a credit balance in the Bank account on the Trial Balance.

questions.question: An unpresented cheque is best described as:
options: A cheque on the bank statement but not yet in the Cash Book
A cheque recorded in the Cash Book as a payment but not yet cleared on the bank statement
A cheque received from a customer but not yet banked
A cancelled or bounced cheque
questions.explanation: An unpresented cheque has been written out and recorded as a payment in the Cash Book, but the bank hasn't processed it yet — so it doesn't appear on the bank statement. It's a timing difference on the payment side.

questions.question: Bank charges appearing on the bank statement should first be recorded in the Cash Book BEFORE preparing the Bank Reconciliation Statement.
questions.explanation: Items on the bank statement not yet in the Cash Book (bank charges, direct debits, interest) must be UPDATED in the Cash Book first. Only then is the revised Cash Book balance used in the Bank Reconciliation Statement.

questions.question: Murphy's Bakery's Cash Book shows a closing balance of €3,838. Bank charges of €25 appear on the statement but not in the Cash Book. What is the revised Cash Book balance after updating?
questions.explanation: Bank charges are a payment not yet recorded in the Cash Book. Updated balance = €3,838 − €25 = €3,813. The balance decreases because bank charges reduce the bank balance.

questions.question: Which of the following are TIMING DIFFERENCES shown in the Bank Reconciliation Statement? Select all that apply.
options: Unpresented cheques (in Cash Book, not yet on bank statement)
Bank charges already updated in the Cash Book
Uncleared lodgements (in Cash Book, not yet credited on the bank statement)
Direct debits already recorded in the updated Cash Book
questions.explanation: Timing differences in the BRS are items that exist in one record but not yet the other. Unpresented cheques (A) and uncleared lodgements (C) are the classic examples. Items already updated in the Cash Book (B, D) are already reconciled — they don't appear in the BRS.

questions.question: Murphy's Bakery's bank statement shows a closing balance of €4,613. Cheque 104 for €800 (rent) is unpresented. Starting from the bank statement balance, calculate the reconciled amount.
questions.explanation: Bank statement balance €4,613 − Unpresented Cheque 104 €800 = €3,813. This should match the updated Cash Book balance of €3,813, confirming the reconciliation is complete.

questions.question: An uncleared lodgement (deposit in transit) is ADDED to the bank statement balance when preparing the Bank Reconciliation Statement.
questions.explanation: The standard format: Bank Statement Balance + Uncleared Lodgements − Unpresented Cheques = Updated Cash Book Balance. Uncleared lodgements are recorded in the Cash Book but not yet credited by the bank, so they're added to the statement balance to reconcile.

questions.question: When a bank reconciliation does not balance after updating the Cash Book, you should:
options: Adjust the bank statement balance until they match
Systematically check both the Cash Book and bank statement for missing entries or transposition errors
Issue a correcting cheque to cover the difference
Contact Revenue to report the discrepancy
questions.explanation: When the reconciliation fails, you work through both records methodically — checking for missing entries, transpositions, or double-postings. The bank statement is the authoritative external record; errors are in the books.

questions.question: On the VAT3 return, T1 represents:
options: Total VAT on purchases (Input VAT)
Total VAT on sales and supplies (Output VAT)
Net VAT payable to Revenue
Total sales excluding VAT
questions.explanation: T1 = Total sales excluding VAT (net turnover). T3 = Output VAT (VAT charged on sales). T4 = Input VAT (VAT on purchases). T5 = T3 − T4 when T3 > T4 (VAT payable). T6 = T4 − T3 when T4 > T3 (VAT refundable).

questions.question: VAT on sales returns (credit notes issued to customers) increases the Output VAT (T3) figure on the VAT return.
questions.explanation: Sales returns REDUCE Output VAT. When a credit note is issued, VAT is refunded to the customer, so T3 decreases. Correct formula: T3 = VAT on sales + VAT on cash sales − VAT on sales returns.

questions.question: Murphy's Bakery VAT Return for March: Sales Day Book VAT €322; Cash sales VAT €92; Sales Returns VAT €23; Purchases Day Book VAT €184. Calculate VAT payable to Revenue.
questions.explanation: Output VAT (T3) = 322 + 92 − 23 = 391. Input VAT (T4) = 184. VAT payable (T5) = T3 − T4 = 391 − 184 = €207 payable to Revenue.

questions.question: Murphy's Bakery makes a cash sale for €492 gross (VAT already included at 23%). What is the VAT amount (Output VAT / T3 contribution)?
questions.explanation: VAT included in a gross price = Gross × 23/123 = €492 × 23/123 = €92. Never multiply the gross by 23% directly — that gives €113.16 (wrong). The 23/123 formula extracts VAT from a VAT-inclusive price.

questions.question: Which of the following are included as POSITIVE additions to Output VAT (T3) on the VAT return? Select all that apply.
options: VAT on credit sales invoices
VAT on cash sales lodged to the bank
VAT on purchases from suppliers
VAT on sales returns (credit notes issued)
questions.explanation: T3 (Output VAT) is VAT charged on sales — both credit sales and cash sales are positive contributions. VAT on purchases goes to T4 (Input VAT — a separate figure). VAT on sales returns REDUCES T3 (deducted, not added).

questions.question: A VAT-registered business must charge VAT on all goods and services it supplies, regardless of the nature of those goods or services.
questions.explanation: Not all supplies attract VAT. Some are exempt (financial services, education, medical), others are zero-rated (most food, children's clothes), and some are at reduced rates (e.g., 13.5% for construction). Only standard-rated and reduced-rate supplies result in a VAT charge.

questions.question: A purchase invoice shows a gross (VAT-inclusive) amount of €1,230 at 23% VAT. What is the Input VAT (T4) amount that can be reclaimed?
questions.explanation: Input VAT = Gross × 23/123 = €1,230 × 23/123 = €230. The net purchase value is €1,000. Always use ×23/123 to extract VAT from a gross figure.

questions.question: When setting up a new company in Sage 50, which must be configured FIRST before entering any transactions?
options: Customer contact details
Chart of accounts and opening balances
The current bank statement
A backup schedule
questions.explanation: The chart of accounts is the structured list of all ledger codes (assets, liabilities, income, expenses). Opening balances bring the system up to date. Without these, transactions cannot be correctly categorised.

questions.question: In Sage 50, entering a sales invoice automatically posts to the customer's account, the sales nominal account, AND the VAT control account — all from a single entry.
questions.explanation: This is the core advantage of integrated accounting software. One posted invoice simultaneously updates the debtors ledger, the sales account, and VAT control — no separate postings needed.

questions.question: Which of the following represent best practice for data backup in a computerised accounting system? Select all that apply.
options: Keep at least one backup copy offsite or in the cloud
Back up after every processing session or daily
Store all backups on the same hard drive as the software
Test backups periodically to verify they can actually be restored
questions.explanation: Offsite/cloud backup (A) protects against fire and theft. Daily backups (B) minimise data loss. Testing (D) ensures backups work when needed. Storing all backups on the same drive (C) is dangerous — one hardware failure loses everything.

questions.question: In Sage 50, which report shows all customers and the amounts they currently owe, broken down by age of debt?
options: Nominal Activity Report
Aged Debtors Report
Profit and Loss Account
Bank Reconciliation Report
questions.explanation: The Aged Debtors Report lists all customers with outstanding balances, split by how long each amount has been owed (current, 30, 60, 90+ days). It is the key tool for credit control.

questions.question: When using computerised accounting software, it is impossible to post a transaction to the wrong account because the software automatically assigns the correct account.
questions.explanation: Software prevents mathematical imbalances (debits ≠ credits) but cannot prevent posting to the WRONG account. You can enter a fixed asset as an expense, use the wrong VAT code, or miscategorise income. The software balances — it doesn't verify accounting judgement.

questions.question: In Sage 50, what feature minimises the risk of posting purchase invoices to the wrong expense account?
options: Nominal Activity Report
Default nominal code set on the supplier record
Trial Balance
Batch entry mode
questions.explanation: Setting a default nominal (expense) code on each supplier record means Sage automatically suggests the correct expense account when posting that supplier's invoices — reducing miscategorisation risk.

questions.question: A business manually processes 120 purchase invoices per month (6 minutes each). Switching to Sage 50 reduces this to 2 minutes per invoice. How many hours per month are saved?
questions.explanation: Time saved per invoice = 6 − 2 = 4 minutes. Total saved = 120 × 4 = 480 minutes ÷ 60 = 8 hours per month.

questions.question: A transaction that has been completely missed from the books (not recorded on either side) is an example of:
options: Error of Commission
Error of Omission
Error of Principle
Compensating Error
questions.explanation: An Error of Omission: the transaction is never entered at all. The Trial Balance still balances (both sides are missing equally), but the accounts are incomplete and don't reflect the full picture.

questions.question: An error of principle occurs when a transaction is posted to the correct type of account but in the wrong amount.
questions.explanation: An error of principle means posting to the WRONG TYPE of account — e.g., treating a fixed asset purchase as an expense. Posting the right type but wrong amount is an 'error of original entry'. Error type is about account category, not amount.

questions.question: A €15,000 purchase of a motor vehicle was accidentally posted to Repairs & Maintenance. The correcting journal entry is:
options: DR Repairs & Maintenance / CR Motor Vehicles €15,000
DR Motor Vehicles / CR Repairs & Maintenance €15,000
DR Sales / CR Motor Vehicles €15,000
DR Motor Vehicles / CR Bank €15,000
questions.explanation: To correct: remove from the wrong account (CR Repairs & Maintenance to reduce it) and put it in the correct account (DR Motor Vehicles). This is the standard journal entry for correcting an error of principle.

questions.question: Which errors would NOT be detected by an out-of-balance Trial Balance? Select all that apply.
options: Error of omission (transaction completely missed)
Error of commission (wrong account, same account type)
Single-sided posting (debit with no credit)
Compensating errors (two equal and opposite errors)
questions.explanation: Errors of omission (A), commission (B), and compensating errors (D) all leave the TB balanced. Only a single-sided posting (C) creates an imbalance — one side of the entry is missing entirely.

questions.question: A Suspense Account with a remaining balance on the Trial Balance indicates there is at least one unresolved error in the books.
questions.explanation: A Suspense Account temporarily holds entries where the correct account is unclear, or balances the TB while errors are investigated. An uncleared balance means an error (or errors totalling that amount) remains unresolved. It must be cleared before preparing final accounts.

questions.question: A payment of €450 to a supplier was posted as €540 on both sides (cash book and creditor's account). What is the amount of the correcting journal entry?
questions.explanation: Overstatement = €540 − €450 = €90. Correction: DR Creditors Account €90 (reducing the overstatement) / CR Cash Book €90. The correcting amount is the difference, not the full original amount.

questions.question: A sales invoice for €600 to City Café was posted to Hotel Plaza's account by mistake. This is an example of:
options: Error of Principle
Error of Omission
Error of Commission
Error of Original Entry
questions.explanation: An Error of Commission: posted to the CORRECT TYPE of account (debtors ledger) but the WRONG specific account within that type. Both City Café and Hotel Plaza are debtors — the account type is right, but the wrong person was charged.

questions.question: Which report shows all income and expenses over a period, resulting in a net profit or loss?
options: Balance Sheet
Aged Creditors Report
Profit and Loss Account (Income Statement)
Trial Balance
questions.explanation: The Profit and Loss Account (Income Statement) shows: Revenue − Cost of Sales = Gross Profit; Gross Profit − Expenses = Net Profit/Loss. It covers a period of time. The Balance Sheet is a snapshot of assets/liabilities/capital at a point in time.

questions.question: The Balance Sheet is sometimes described as a 'snapshot' because it shows the financial position of the business at a specific point in time, not over a period.
questions.explanation: The Balance Sheet (Statement of Financial Position) shows what the business OWNS (assets) and OWES (liabilities + capital) on ONE specific date. Unlike the P&L (which covers a trading period), it is a point-in-time picture.

questions.question: Which of the following reports would a credit controller use to manage overdue customer payments? Select all that apply.
options: Aged Debtors Report
Customer Statements
Aged Creditors Report
Profit and Loss Account
questions.explanation: The Aged Debtors Report (A) shows which customers owe money and how long it's been outstanding. Customer Statements (B) are sent to customers to show all transactions and the balance due — both are credit control tools. Aged Creditors (C) is about what the business owes; P&L (D) shows income/expenses.

questions.question: Under GDPR, a business storing payroll records must:
options: Delete all payroll records after 12 months
Store them securely and not retain them longer than legally required
Keep them indefinitely in case of future audit
Share them with third parties on request without restriction
questions.explanation: GDPR requires personal data (including payroll) to be stored securely, processed lawfully, and not kept longer than necessary. Irish tax law requires retention for at least 6 years; after that, records should be securely destroyed.

questions.question: A VAT-registered business in Ireland must retain records relating to VAT for a minimum of 6 years.
questions.explanation: Revenue requires all books of account, invoices, and VAT records to be retained for a minimum of 6 years from the date of the last transaction to which they relate. This applies to VAT, payroll, and financial records.

questions.question: A business has net profit of €28,500 and total revenue of €95,000. What is the Net Profit Margin as a percentage?
questions.explanation: Net Profit Margin = (Net Profit ÷ Revenue) × 100 = (€28,500 ÷ €95,000) × 100 = 30%. This KPI shows how much of each euro of revenue ends up as profit after all costs.

questions.question: An audit trail in an accounting system is best described as:
options: A route used by auditors to travel between office locations
A log of every transaction, modification, and deletion with timestamps and user details
A summary report generated at year-end for the auditor
The chronological sequence of journal entries
questions.explanation: An audit trail records ALL data entry, changes, and deletions — who did it, what changed, and when. It enables management and auditors to trace any figure back to its source and is essential for fraud prevention and regulatory compliance.

questions.question: Gross pay is defined as:
options: An employee's take-home pay after all deductions
Total earnings before any deductions
Basic pay excluding overtime and bonuses
Net pay plus employer PRSI
questions.explanation: Gross pay is TOTAL earnings before any deductions (PAYE, PRSI, USC, pension). Net pay (take-home) is what the employee actually receives AFTER all deductions are made.

questions.question: Employer PRSI is a cost deducted from the employee's gross pay.
questions.explanation: Employer PRSI is an ADDITIONAL cost paid by the employer ON TOP of the employee's gross pay — it is NOT deducted from the employee's wages. It is the employer's own social insurance contribution, separate from the employee's PRSI deduction.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a P60 form?
options: Issued when an employee leaves, summarising final pay
An annual certificate showing total pay, PAYE, PRSI, and USC for the tax year
Monthly return filed by the employer with Revenue
Form used to claim a tax refund directly
questions.explanation: A P60 is issued by an employer to each employee at year-end (by 15 February). It summarises total gross pay, PAYE, PRSI, and USC for the full tax year. Employees use it for mortgage applications, tax refund claims, and social welfare.

questions.question: Which of the following are deducted from an employee's gross pay to arrive at net pay? Select all that apply.
options: PAYE (Income Tax)
Employee PRSI
USC (Universal Social Charge)
Employer PRSI
questions.explanation: PAYE, employee PRSI, and USC are all deducted FROM gross pay. Employer PRSI is paid BY the employer as an additional cost on top of gross pay — it does not reduce the employee's take-home pay.

questions.question: A tax credit of €1,000 reduces an employee's taxable income by €1,000.
questions.explanation: Tax credits reduce the TAX PAYABLE (not taxable income) by the full credit amount. A €1,000 tax credit reduces the PAYE bill by €1,000 — euro for euro. This is more valuable than a tax relief of the same amount, which only reduces income subject to tax.

questions.question: An employee earns €800 gross per week. Their weekly tax credit is €40 and all income is at the standard rate of 20%. Calculate their weekly PAYE (income tax) payable.
questions.explanation: Gross PAYE = €800 × 20% = €160. Less tax credit: €160 − €40 = €120 PAYE payable. Tax credits are deducted AFTER applying the rate to gross pay.

questions.question: A P45 form is issued:
options: Monthly by all employers to Revenue
To an employee when they leave employment
Annually to all employees at the end of the tax year
When an employee has emergency tax applied
questions.explanation: A P45 is issued when an employee LEAVES employment. It shows their pay and deductions to the date of leaving. The employee gives Part 2 & 3 to their new employer to avoid emergency tax and be correctly set up on the cumulative basis.

questions.question: Thesaurus Payroll and Sage Payroll are examples of:
options: Bookkeeping software packages
Spreadsheet applications
Specialist payroll software packages
HR management systems only
questions.explanation: Thesaurus Payroll (widely used in Ireland) and Sage Payroll are dedicated payroll software packages. They calculate PAYE, PRSI, and USC automatically, generate payslips, and produce Revenue returns via ROS.

questions.question: Payroll software automatically submits P30 returns directly to Revenue without any employer action required.
questions.explanation: Payroll software GENERATES the return and can export the data in the correct format, but the employer or their accountant must still log in to ROS and submit it. Software doesn't autonomously submit to Revenue without human action.

questions.question: The primary advantage of payroll software over manual calculation is:
options: It eliminates the need to keep payroll records
Automatic, accurate calculation of PAYE, PRSI, and USC using current tax tables
It never makes errors
Employees can edit their own payslip details
questions.explanation: The core advantage is automatic calculation using current tax tables, eliminating manual arithmetic errors and ensuring compliance with correct rates and bands.

questions.question: Which of the following are typically shown on a computerised payslip? Select all that apply.
options: Employee name and PPS number
Gross pay breakdown (basic, overtime, bonus)
Employer's PRSI contribution (for information)
Net pay (take-home amount)
questions.explanation: All four items typically appear on a payslip. PPS number identifies the employee. Gross pay breakdown shows the composition of pay. Employer PRSI is shown for transparency (though not deducted from the employee). Net pay is what the employee takes home.

questions.question: Payroll software can automatically generate the payroll journal entry (DR Wages / CR PAYE Payable / CR PRSI Payable / CR Bank) for import into the accounting system.
questions.explanation: Most payroll software can export the payroll journal in a format compatible with accounting packages like Sage 50. This eliminates manual posting and reduces the risk of bookkeeping errors following each payroll run.

questions.question: A business pays 8 employees weekly. Manual payroll takes 25 minutes per payslip. Payroll software processes the full payroll run in 15 minutes total. How many minutes per week are saved?
questions.explanation: Manual time = 8 × 25 = 200 minutes. Software time = 15 minutes. Saving = 200 − 15 = 185 minutes per week.

questions.question: An employee starts a new job and provides their P45. The employer should:
options: Automatically place them on emergency tax
Set up the employee on the cumulative basis using the year-to-date figures from the P45
Apply for a tax refund on their behalf
Register them for USC exemption
questions.explanation: The P45 contains the employee's year-to-date pay and deductions. The new employer uses this to set up the employee on the cumulative basis, carrying forward the YTD figures so the correct tax is applied for the remainder of the year.

questions.question: In the cumulative tax system, tax each period is calculated by:
options: Taxing each week's earnings independently with no reference to prior periods
Applying 40% to all earnings above the cut-off regardless of year-to-date amounts
Using year-to-date totals for pay, tax credits, and band — then deducting tax already paid
Averaging gross pay over the full year and taxing the average
questions.explanation: The cumulative system uses year-to-date (YTD) totals. Each period: calculate tax on cumulative gross pay to date, apply cumulative credits and cut-off to date, then deduct tax already paid in previous periods. This self-corrects automatically if pay changes.

questions.question: Under the cumulative tax system, if too much tax was deducted in January, this is automatically corrected (refunded) in February's payroll.
questions.explanation: Because the cumulative system calculates tax on YTD figures and deducts what was already paid, an overpayment in one month will show as a negative (refund) in the next payroll run — automatically, without the employee filing a claim.

questions.question: An employee earns €3,500 per month. Their monthly standard rate cut-off is €3,333. How much of their monthly income is taxed at the higher 40% rate?
options: €0 — all income is at the standard rate
questions.explanation: Income up to €3,333 is taxed at 20% (standard rate). Income ABOVE the cut-off: €3,500 − €3,333 = €167 is taxed at 40% (higher rate). Only the excess beyond the cut-off attracts the higher rate.

questions.question: Which of the following are features of the cumulative tax system? Select all that apply.
options: Year-to-date pay is used to calculate each period's tax liability
Overpaid tax in one period is automatically refunded in the next
Each pay period is taxed independently with no reference to prior periods
Unused tax credits from earlier in the year can reduce tax due later in the year
questions.explanation: The cumulative system uses YTD figures (A), self-corrects (B), and allows earlier unused credits to offset tax later (D). Independent period taxation (C) describes Week 1/Month 1 (emergency tax), not the cumulative system.

questions.question: Under Ireland's two-rate income tax system, income below the standard rate cut-off is taxed at 20%, and income above it is taxed at 40%.
questions.explanation: Ireland operates a two-rate system: 20% (standard rate) up to the cut-off, and 40% (higher rate) on income above it. The exact cut-off depends on personal circumstances (single, married, etc.) and is updated in each annual Budget.

questions.question: An employee's monthly gross pay is €4,000. Monthly standard rate cut-off is €3,500. Calculate their total monthly gross PAYE (before deducting tax credits) using 20% standard and 40% higher rate.
An employee receives a large bonus in October. Under the cumulative tax system, the bonus will:
options: Always be taxed entirely at the higher 40% rate
Automatically reflect the employee's actual YTD earnings, potentially pushing more income into the higher rate band
Be exempt from PAYE as it is a once-off payment
Require the employee to file a self-assessment return
questions.explanation: The cumulative system accounts for YTD earnings. A bonus increases cumulative gross pay — this may push earnings over the standard rate cut-off if not already there, or increase the higher-rate portion. It is not automatically all taxed at 40%.

questions.question: Emergency tax is applied when:
options: An employee earns more than the standard rate cut-off
An employer has not received the employee's PPS number or Revenue has not issued a Tax Credit Certificate
An employee has worked for less than 13 weeks
An employee misses a monthly payroll deduction payment
questions.explanation: Emergency tax is applied when the employer cannot obtain the employee's Revenue credentials. Without a PPS number or Tax Credit Certificate, the employer applies emergency (Week 1/Month 1) rates to ensure some tax is collected while the situation is regularised.

questions.question: Under the Week 1 / Month 1 emergency tax basis, each pay period is taxed independently — past overpayments or underpayments are NOT automatically corrected.
questions.explanation: Emergency (Week 1/Month 1) tax treats each period in isolation. Year-to-date figures are NOT used, so there is no automatic correction of earlier periods. This is less favourable than cumulative tax for the employee if they've been overpaying.

questions.question: If an employee is on emergency tax and has not provided their PPS number, after how many weeks does the employer apply the higher 40% rate to ALL earnings?
options: After 2 weeks (Week 3 onwards)
After 4 weeks (Week 5 onwards)
After 8 weeks
After 13 weeks
questions.explanation: For the first 4 weeks (Weeks 1–4) of emergency tax, the standard rate applies with no tax credits. From Week 5 onwards, if no PPS number has been provided, the employer must apply the higher rate (40%) to ALL earnings with no credits.

questions.question: Which situations would result in an employee being placed on emergency tax? Select all that apply.
options: New employee who has not provided a P45 or PPS number
Employee returning from maternity leave to the same employer
Employee whose Tax Credit Certificate has expired and no new one issued
Employee starting their very first job with no employment history
questions.explanation: Emergency tax applies when Revenue credentials are unavailable. New employees without P45 (A), those without a valid Tax Credit Certificate (C), and first-time workers (D) with no Revenue record all trigger emergency tax. Returning from maternity leave (B) retains their existing tax status — no emergency tax.

questions.question: Once an employee on emergency tax provides their PPS number and Revenue issues a Tax Credit Certificate, any tax overpaid will be automatically refunded through subsequent payrolls.
questions.explanation: Once regularised, the employer switches to the cumulative basis. The system compares YTD tax paid vs. what should have been paid — if the employee overpaid, the cumulative system generates a refund in the next payroll, automatically.

questions.question: In Week 3 of emergency tax (PPS number not provided, standard 20% rate applies, no tax credits), an employee earns €650 gross. What is their PAYE for that week?
questions.explanation: Weeks 1–4 emergency tax (without PPS): 20% applied to all earnings, no tax credits. PAYE = €650 × 20% = €130.

questions.question: To resolve an employee's emergency tax situation, the employer needs to:
options: Apply for a rebate from Revenue on the employee's behalf
Register the employee on ROS using their PPS number so Revenue issues a Tax Credit Certificate
Switch them permanently to the 40% higher rate
File a P30 adjustment for the affected months
questions.explanation: The employer registers the new employee on Revenue's PMOD system via ROS using their PPS number. Revenue then issues a Tax Credit Certificate enabling the employer to switch the employee to the cumulative basis with correct credits.

questions.question: When an employee gets married and they or their spouse is a lower earner or non-worker, the main PAYE benefit of joint assessment is:
options: A fixed marriage tax credit of €5,000
Transfer of unused tax credits and standard rate cut-off from the non-working or lower-earning spouse
Exemption from USC for both spouses
Both spouses pay at the lower 20% rate on all income
questions.explanation: Under joint assessment, unused tax credits and standard rate cut-off from a non-working or lower-earning spouse can be transferred to the higher earner, reducing their PAYE. This is the main financial advantage of marriage for tax purposes.

questions.question: Under the Irish income tax system, a single-income married couple taxed under joint assessment can transfer the Employee (PAYE) Tax Credit from the non-working spouse to the working spouse.
questions.explanation: The Employee (PAYE) Tax Credit is specifically for PAYE workers. It CANNOT be transferred between spouses — it must be earned through PAYE employment. The Personal Tax Credit (and some others) CAN be transferred.

questions.question: An employee is made redundant. Which element of a redundancy payment is exempt from PAYE under the Basic Exemption?
options: All redundancy payments are fully taxable
The first €10,160 plus €765 per full year of service of the ex-gratia (additional) payment
Only statutory redundancy, not additional payments
The first €5,000 of any redundancy payment
questions.explanation: The Basic Exemption (€10,160 + €765 per full year of service) applies to ex-gratia (additional) redundancy payments. Statutory redundancy (paid under the Redundancy Payments Acts) is fully exempt from income tax in all cases.

questions.question: Which personal changes should be notified to Revenue to update tax credits? Select all that apply.
options: Getting married or entering a civil partnership
Claiming the Single Person Child Carer Credit after separation
Receiving a pay rise
A dependent relative becoming incapacitated (Dependent Relative Credit)
questions.explanation: Marriage (A), child carer credits (B), and dependent relative credits (D) all change tax credits or cut-off that Revenue assigns. A pay rise (C) affects gross pay but NOT the tax credit or cut-off — the cumulative system handles higher earnings automatically.

questions.question: If an employee starts a new job mid-year without a P45, the new employer must apply emergency (Week 1/Month 1) tax until Revenue issues a Tax Credit Certificate.
questions.explanation: Without a P45, the new employer has no YTD figures and can't operate the cumulative basis. The employee is placed on emergency tax until Revenue issues a Tax Credit Certificate following ROS registration by the employer.

questions.question: An employee earns €38,000 gross annually. Standard rate cut-off is €42,000. Annual tax credits are €3,600. All income is below the cut-off (20% standard rate). Calculate annual PAYE.
questions.explanation: Gross PAYE = €38,000 × 20% = €7,600. Net PAYE = €7,600 − €3,600 (credits) = €4,000 annual PAYE payable.

questions.question: In the year of marriage, spouses are taxed as single persons for the full year. However, they may claim:
options: An automatic marriage bonus from Revenue
A refund if they would have paid less tax had they been jointly assessed for the married portion of the year
Exemption from USC for the year of marriage
A reduction in employer PRSI for 12 months
questions.explanation: Year of Marriage relief: Revenue calculates tax on both a single-person and married-couple basis. If the couple would have paid less under joint assessment for the married period, Revenue refunds the difference.

questions.question: An employer must issue a P60 to each employee in employment on 31 December by:
options: 31 December of the same year
15 February of the following year
31 March of the following year
Within 7 days of year-end
questions.explanation: P60s must be issued by 15 February following the end of the tax year (31 December). It summarises total gross pay, PAYE, PRSI, and USC for the full year for each employee who was employed on 31 December.

questions.question: An employee who leaves employment mid-year receives both a P45 on leaving AND a P60 at year-end from their former employer.
questions.explanation: An employee who leaves during the year receives a P45 on leaving. Since they are NOT in employment on 31 December, they do NOT receive a P60 from that employer. Their P45 provides the year-to-date summary.

questions.question: When a P45 is issued to a leaving employee, which part does the employee give to their new employer?
options: Part 1 only
Part 2 only
Part 3, with Part 2 retained by the employee for their own records
Parts 1 and 2
questions.explanation: Part 1 goes to Revenue. Part 2 is the employee's own copy to keep. Part 3 is given to the new employer to set up the correct cumulative tax basis. The employee retains Part 2 as personal evidence.

questions.question: For which of the following purposes can an employee use their P60? Select all that apply.
options: Applying for a mortgage (annual income verification)
Claiming a PAYE tax refund if too much was deducted
Social welfare means test requiring annual income proof
Setting up their tax basis with a new employer mid-year
questions.explanation: P60 is used for income verification (mortgages), tax refund claims, and social welfare means tests. It is NOT used to set up with a new employer mid-year — that requires the P45 (which has current-year YTD figures). P60 covers a completed prior tax year.

questions.question: An employer must issue a P45 on the actual day an employee gives notice of resignation.
questions.explanation: A P45 is issued when the employee ACTUALLY LEAVES (after their final pay date). It cannot be issued in advance because it must include final, accurate pay figures. Issuing it before the last payroll would make the figures incorrect.

questions.question: An employee's P60 shows total PAYE deducted of €4,200. Revenue calculates that only €3,800 should have been deducted. How much tax refund is the employee entitled to?
questions.explanation: Refund = Actual deductions − Correct deductions = €4,200 − €3,800 = €400. The employee can claim this refund from Revenue using the P60 and myAccount.

questions.question: Under PAYE Modernisation (introduced in Ireland in 2019), what replaced the traditional P45 system?
options: A digital P60 filed directly with Revenue by the employer
Real-time payroll reporting — employers report each employee's pay and deductions to Revenue on or before each pay date via ROS
Monthly P30 returns that include individual employee details
Annual P35 employer summary reports
questions.explanation: PAYE Modernisation introduced Real Time Reporting (RTR). Each pay period, employers submit payroll data to Revenue via ROS. This replaced the P45 (and P35) with real-time data — Revenue now has up-to-date information without waiting for year-end returns.

questions.question: A P30 is:
options: A form issued to employees when they leave employment
A monthly or quarterly employer return to Revenue showing PAYE/PRSI/USC deducted and employer PRSI
An annual employee tax certificate
A form for claiming a USC exemption
questions.explanation: The P30 is the employer's periodic return (monthly or quarterly) to Revenue reporting all PAYE, employee PRSI, employer PRSI, and USC from payroll. Payment is typically due by the 23rd of the following month.

questions.question: Under PAYE Modernisation, smaller employers may be permitted to file payroll tax returns quarterly rather than monthly.
questions.explanation: Small employers (whose monthly PAYE/PRSI/USC liability falls below Revenue's threshold) may file and pay quarterly rather than monthly. Larger employers must file and pay monthly. Revenue determines eligibility.

questions.question: What are the consequences for an employer who fails to file a P30 return by the Revenue deadline?
options: No penalty — Revenue will simply calculate the amount owed
Interest charges on the outstanding amount plus potential fixed penalties; Revenue may issue an estimated assessment
Automatic suspension of the business's trading licence
The employer's VAT registration is immediately cancelled
questions.explanation: Late P30 filing attracts daily interest charges (currently 0.0219% per day) on the outstanding amount plus possible fixed penalties. Revenue may also raise an estimated assessment. Repeated non-compliance risks audit or enforcement action.

questions.question: Which of the following are included in the P30 return filed by an employer? Select all that apply.
options: Total PAYE (income tax) deducted from all employees
Total employee PRSI contributions
Total employer PRSI contributions
Total USC deducted from employees
questions.explanation: The P30 covers ALL payroll-related liabilities owed to Revenue: PAYE, employee PRSI, employer PRSI, and USC. The employer is responsible for deducting and remitting all of these.

questions.question: Under the pre-2019 payroll system, employers were required to file an annual P35 return summarising every employee's pay and deductions for the full tax year.
questions.explanation: The P35 was an annual employer return listing all employees with their annual gross pay, PAYE, PRSI, and USC totals. It was abolished under PAYE Modernisation in 2019 because Revenue now receives this data in real time through payroll submissions.

questions.question: An employer processes monthly payroll: total PAYE €3,200; employee PRSI €740; employer PRSI €2,035; USC €520. What is the total P30 payment due to Revenue?
questions.explanation: Total P30 = PAYE + Employee PRSI + Employer PRSI + USC = €3,200 + €740 + €2,035 + €520 = €6,495. The employer remits this full amount to Revenue (not just the employee deductions).

questions.question: Revenue's ROS (Revenue Online Service) is used by employers for:
options: Automating all payroll calculations
Filing P30 returns, paying PAYE/PRSI/USC liabilities, submitting payroll data, and accessing employer tax records
Applying for employee tax credit certificates on the employee's behalf
VAT registration only
questions.explanation: ROS is Revenue's online portal for businesses. Employers use it to file returns, pay liabilities, submit real-time payroll data under PAYE Modernisation, and access tax records. It is not a payroll calculation tool — that is payroll software's role.

questions.question: Which method of married couples' tax assessment is most common and generally most tax-efficient?
options: Single Assessment
Separate Assessment
Joint Assessment
Dual Assessment
questions.explanation: Joint Assessment (Aggregation) is the most common and usually most beneficial option. One spouse is the assessable spouse, income and credits are pooled, and unused credits/cut-off can be transferred between spouses.

questions.question: Under Single Assessment, married spouses are taxed completely independently with no transfer of tax credits or standard rate cut-off between them.
questions.explanation: Single assessment means each spouse is treated exactly as a single person for tax — no transfers are permitted. This is the least common option for married couples and is rarely beneficial unless both spouses have high incomes and don't want their affairs combined.

questions.question: The Home Carer Tax Credit is available when:
options: Any married person works from home
One spouse stays home to care for a dependent child or incapacitated person, and the home carer's own income is below the threshold
Both spouses work part-time to share childcare
An employer provides childcare facilities
questions.explanation: The Home Carer Credit is specifically for married couples where one spouse is at home caring for a dependent (child or incapacitated adult) and their own income is below a specified limit. It recognises the economic value of caring.

questions.question: Under joint assessment, which of the following can typically be transferred from a non-working spouse to the working spouse? Select all that apply.
options: Personal Tax Credit
Unused portion of the standard rate cut-off
Employee (PAYE) Tax Credit
Age Tax Credit (where applicable)
questions.explanation: The Personal Tax Credit (A), unused standard rate cut-off (B), and Age Tax Credit (D) can be transferred under joint assessment. The Employee (PAYE) Tax Credit (C) cannot be transferred — it is only available to those in PAYE employment themselves.

questions.question: If a married couple opts for joint assessment and only one spouse earns income, that spouse can use both spouses' Personal Tax Credits.
questions.explanation: Under joint assessment, the assessable spouse claims both Personal Tax Credits — their own and their non-working spouse's (who has no income to use it against). This significantly reduces the working spouse's PAYE bill.

questions.question: Under joint assessment, a working spouse earns €55,000. Combined Personal Tax Credits = €3,750. Combined standard rate cut-off = €51,000. Tax rates: 20% up to cut-off, 40% above. Calculate annual PAYE.
In the year of marriage, what is the 'Year of Marriage' tax relief?
options: A fixed €2,000 credit in the year of marriage
A refund if the couple would have paid less tax under joint assessment for the married portion of the year
Exemption from USC for the marriage year
A reduction in the higher rate band for two years
questions.explanation: Revenue calculates tax on both a single-person basis (how it was actually taxed) and a joint assessment basis for the period after marriage. If joint assessment would have resulted in lower tax, Revenue refunds the difference.

questions.question: The National Minimum Wage in Ireland is:
options: Set once and never changed
Recommended annually by the Low Pay Commission and applies to most employees
Calculated differently for each industry by IBEC
Only applicable to part-time employees
questions.explanation: The Low Pay Commission reviews and recommends the National Minimum Wage annually. It applies to most adult employees in Ireland. Sub-minimum rates apply to those under 20. Both public and private sector workers are covered (though many earn above it).

questions.question: Under the Organisation of Working Time Act 1997, employees in Ireland cannot be required to work an average of more than 48 hours per week (averaged over a reference period).
questions.explanation: The Organisation of Working Time Act 1997 (implementing an EU Working Time Directive) sets a maximum average working week of 48 hours. Unlike the UK, Ireland does not allow employees to opt out of this limit.

questions.question: Which body investigates complaints about breaches of employment rights in Ireland (e.g., minimum wage, unfair dismissal)?
options: Revenue Commissioners
The Workplace Relations Commission (WRC)
The Health and Safety Authority (HSA)
The Data Protection Commission (DPC)
questions.explanation: The Workplace Relations Commission (WRC) handles complaints under employment rights legislation — minimum wage, working hours, unfair dismissal, equality, and more. It replaced NERA (National Employment Rights Authority) in 2015.

questions.question: Which of the following are statutory employment rights protected under Irish legislation? Select all that apply.
options: Entitlement to paid annual leave (minimum 4 weeks)
Protection against unfair dismissal (after qualifying period)
Right to rest breaks during the working day
Right to earn above the National Minimum Wage
questions.explanation: Annual leave (A), unfair dismissal protection (B), and rest break entitlements (C) are all statutory rights. There is no right to earn MORE than the minimum wage (D) — that is a matter of employment contract negotiation, not legislation.

questions.question: Under GDPR, an employer processing employee payroll data must ensure it is accurate, securely stored, and not retained longer than necessary.
questions.explanation: GDPR requires personal data — including payroll, tax, and employee details — to be: lawfully processed, accurate, securely stored, and not kept longer than necessary. Employers must have a legal basis for processing (employment contract) and a clear data retention policy.

questions.question: An employee is entitled to 20 days annual leave per year and has taken 12 days so far. How many days of leave remain?
questions.explanation: Remaining leave = Entitlement − Used = 20 − 12 = 8 days. The Organisation of Working Time Act entitles full-time employees to a minimum of 4 working weeks (20 days) of paid annual leave per year.

questions.question: The Payment of Wages Act 1991 requires employers to:
options: Pay all employees by bank transfer only
Provide a written payslip with each payment showing gross pay, all deductions, and net pay
Pay wages at least fortnightly
Pay a minimum notice period of 4 weeks
questions.explanation: The Payment of Wages Act 1991 requires employers to provide a written payslip (wage slip) to every employee with each payment, detailing gross pay and all deductions. Payment method is not restricted to bank transfer.

questions.question: Which payroll report would a manager use to compare total wage costs this month against the same month last year?
options: Individual payslips
Payroll Summary / Departmental Cost Analysis
Aged Debtors Report
P30 Return
questions.explanation: A Payroll Summary or Departmental Cost Analysis report shows total payroll costs by department or period, enabling period-on-period comparisons. Payslips are per-employee, P30 is a Revenue return, and Aged Debtors relates to customers.

questions.question: Under GDPR, payroll records must be destroyed after 3 years.
questions.explanation: Revenue requires payroll records to be retained for at least 6 YEARS. GDPR requires data not be kept LONGER than necessary — after the 6-year Revenue retention period expires, records should then be securely destroyed. 3 years is not the correct figure.

questions.question: The purpose of an audit trail in a payroll system is:
options: To generate payslips automatically each pay period
To record all changes to payroll data — who changed it, what was changed, and when
To file P30 returns with Revenue
To calculate employee holiday entitlements
questions.explanation: A payroll audit trail logs every change — edits to employee records, pay rate adjustments, manual overrides, and processed payrolls — with timestamps and user IDs. This is essential for fraud prevention, error investigation, and Revenue compliance.

questions.question: Which of the following are key payroll reports generated at period-end? Select all that apply.
options: Payroll Journal (for posting wages to the general ledger)
P30 / Revenue Return (payroll tax liability summary)
Employee earnings and deductions summary
Petty Cash Book
questions.explanation: The Payroll Journal (A) enables bookkeeping (DR Wages / CR PAYE Payable, PRSI Payable, Bank), the P30 (B) is filed with Revenue, and the earnings summary (C) is a management report. The Petty Cash Book (D) is unrelated to payroll reporting.

questions.question: Cloud-based payroll software eliminates the need for any backup procedures because the provider handles all server-side backups.
questions.explanation: While cloud providers handle server-side backups, this doesn't eliminate ALL employer backup responsibilities. Cloud outages, account access issues, or provider failures can occur. Best practice: maintain your own export/backup copies regardless of where the software lives.

questions.question: A business pays 12 employees monthly. Switching from printed to electronic payslips saves €0.50 per payslip. How much is saved annually (12 months)?
questions.explanation: Annual saving = 12 employees × €0.50 × 12 months = €72. Electronic payslips also reduce admin time and improve record-keeping, but the direct printing cost saving is €72 per year.

questions.question: Under PAYE Modernisation, how frequently must employers report employee pay and deductions to Revenue?
options: Annually, via the P35 return
Monthly, via the P30 return only
On or before each pay date via real-time payroll submission
Quarterly, alongside the VAT3 return
questions.explanation: PAYE Modernisation (January 2019) requires employers to report each payroll run to Revenue ON OR BEFORE each pay date — in real time. This replaced the P45 and P35 annual return with continuous, up-to-date data for Revenue.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the double-entry rule for recording an expense payment by cheque?
options: DR Bank Account / CR Expense Account
DR Expense Account / CR Bank Account
DR Expense Account / CR Capital Account
DR Sales Account / CR Expense Account
questions.explanation: Expenses increase on the debit side. The bank (an asset) decreases on the credit side. DR Expense Account / CR Bank Account is the correct double-entry for any expense paid by cheque or bank transfer.

questions.question: Revenue expenditure is expenditure on fixed assets that will benefit the business for more than one accounting period.
questions.explanation: That describes CAPITAL expenditure (e.g., buying a van or machinery). Revenue expenditure is day-to-day running costs (wages, rent, insurance) that are fully expensed in the period in which they occur.

questions.question: Which accounting concept states that the financial affairs of the business must be kept completely separate from the personal affairs of the owner?
options: Going concern concept
Consistency concept
Entity concept
Prudence concept
questions.explanation: The entity (business entity) concept treats the business as distinct from its owner. Even in a sole trade, the business's assets, liabilities, and transactions are recorded separately from the owner's personal finances.

questions.question: A business has fixed assets of €28,000, current assets of €9,500, and total liabilities of €12,300. What is the owner's capital?
questions.explanation: Total assets = €28,000 + €9,500 = €37,500. Capital = Assets − Liabilities = €37,500 − €12,300 = €25,200.

questions.question: Trade creditors (suppliers owed money) are classified as current liabilities on the Balance Sheet.
questions.explanation: Trade creditors are amounts owed to suppliers, typically due within 30–90 days. Since they are payable within 12 months, they are current liabilities — shown under Current Liabilities on the Balance Sheet.

questions.question: Which of the following are examples of FIXED (non-current) assets? Select all that apply.
options: Motor van
Land and buildings
Trade debtors
Office equipment
questions.explanation: Fixed assets are long-term assets held for use over more than one year: motor van (A), land and buildings (B), and office equipment (D). Trade debtors (C) are current assets — they are expected to convert to cash within 12 months.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'going concern' concept?
options: Assets are recorded at their net realisable value in case of immediate sale
The business will continue operating for the foreseeable future, so assets are not valued at break-up prices
All income must be recorded when cash is received
The owner and the business are treated as the same legal entity
questions.explanation: Going concern assumes the business will continue trading indefinitely. This justifies recording assets at cost (or depreciated cost) rather than fire-sale values. If a business is about to close, the going concern assumption no longer applies and different valuation rules apply.

questions.question: Which of the following is a disadvantage of cloud-based accounting software compared to desktop-installed software?
options: Accessible from any internet-connected device
Ongoing subscription cost and reliance on internet connectivity — no access during outages
Automatic software updates included
Real-time collaboration between multiple users
questions.explanation: Cloud accounting requires continuous internet access — outages, subscription lapses, or provider failure all risk access to your data. Desktop software is available offline. The other options listed are advantages of cloud, not disadvantages.

questions.question: Using Microsoft Excel to record transactions is equivalent to using a dedicated accounting package like Sage 50.
questions.explanation: Excel is a general-purpose spreadsheet with no built-in double-entry structure, no automatic ledger postings, no integrated VAT reports, and no audit trail. Sage 50 is purpose-built with all these features. Excel spreadsheets are also much more prone to formula errors.

questions.question: Sage 50 costs €600 per year. Manual bookkeeping requires 3 extra hours per week at €20/hr (52 weeks). What is the net annual saving from switching to Sage 50?
questions.explanation: Manual extra cost = 3 hrs × €20 × 52 weeks = €3,120/year. Net saving = €3,120 − €600 (software) = €2,520 per year.

questions.question: Which of the following are features of an integrated computerised accounting system? Select all that apply.
options: Automatic double-entry posting from a single transaction entry
VAT report generated automatically from posted transactions
Requires a separate manual ledger to check all entries
Aged Debtors report available at any time
questions.explanation: Integrated accounting software automatically posts double-entry (A), generates VAT reports from the data already entered (B), and provides real-time reports like Aged Debtors (D). Requiring a separate manual ledger (C) defeats the purpose of the system.

questions.question: In Sage 50, the 'Nominal Ledger' refers to:
options: The ledger containing all customer accounts
The ledger containing all supplier accounts
The main general ledger — containing all income, expense, asset, and liability accounts
A special ledger for cash transactions only
questions.explanation: The Nominal Ledger is the master general ledger in Sage 50. It holds all nominal (general) accounts: sales, purchases, expenses, assets, liabilities, and capital. Customer accounts are in the Sales Ledger; supplier accounts are in the Purchase Ledger.

questions.question: In a computerised accounting system, once a transaction is posted, it can never be amended or reversed.
questions.explanation: Corrections can be made by posting reversal journal entries (or in some systems via a controlled edit). All changes are recorded in the audit trail. Unlike handwriting in a ledger, electronic corrections are fully traceable.

questions.question: In Sage 50, which module would you use to allocate a customer payment against their outstanding invoice?
options: Bank module
Purchase Ledger module
Customer (Sales) Ledger module
Nominal Ledger module
questions.explanation: The Customer (Sales) Ledger in Sage 50 holds all customer accounts. Allocating a receipt to an invoice (marking it as paid) is done within the customer's account in this module, reducing their outstanding balance.

questions.question: Murphy's Bakery buys flour on credit from Flour Mills Ltd — Invoice FM23: €500 net + 23% VAT. Which book of first entry records this?
options: Sales Day Book
Purchases Day Book
Cash Book (payments side)
General Journal
questions.explanation: Credit purchases (buying on account) are recorded in the Purchases Day Book. The Cash Book only records cash and bank movements. Flour Mills Ltd has not been paid yet — this is a credit transaction.

questions.question: The Purchases Returns Day Book records goods returned BY customers to the business.
questions.explanation: The Purchases Returns Day Book records goods the BUSINESS returns TO its suppliers (e.g., damaged stock returned to a supplier). Goods returned BY customers are recorded in the Sales Returns Day Book.

questions.question: A supplier invoice shows a gross (VAT-inclusive) total of €615 at 23% VAT. What is the net amount (excluding VAT)?
questions.explanation: Net = Gross ÷ 1.23 = €615 ÷ 1.23 = €500. Never simply subtract 23% from the gross — that gives the wrong answer. Divide by 1.23 to correctly extract the net from a VAT-inclusive price.

questions.question: Which of the following are appropriate petty cash expenditures? Select all that apply.
options: €3 bus fare for a courier delivery
€5 postage stamps
€1,200 purchase of office laptop
€8 cleaning supplies
questions.explanation: Petty cash is for small, day-to-day expenses: bus fares, stamps, and small supplies (A, B, D). A laptop costing €1,200 (C) is too large for petty cash — it should be paid by cheque/bank transfer and treated as a capital purchase.

questions.question: The Cash Book serves as both a book of first entry AND a ledger account. Which specific ledger account does it replace?
options: The Sales account in the Nominal Ledger
The Bank account in the Nominal Ledger
The Debtors Control account
The Petty Cash account
questions.explanation: The Cash Book functions as the Bank account (and cash account) within the Nominal Ledger. There is no separate Bank account in the Nominal Ledger when a Cash Book is used — the Cash Book IS the bank account.

questions.question: Cash purchases (paid immediately at point of sale) are recorded in the Purchases Day Book.
questions.explanation: The Purchases Day Book records only CREDIT purchases. Cash purchases are recorded in the Cash Book on the payments side — money leaves the bank immediately, so there is no creditor to record.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a folio reference (e.g., 'SDB p.3') in the day books and ledgers?
options: To calculate the total VAT for the period
To provide a cross-reference linking each entry to the corresponding posting in the ledger
To sequentially number transactions for Revenue audit purposes
To record who processed each transaction
questions.explanation: Folio references provide a cross-referencing trail. A folio in the day book shows where the corresponding double-entry was posted in the ledger, and vice versa. This makes it easy to trace any entry and verify the double-entry was completed.

questions.question: Murphy's Bakery pays rent of €1,200 by cheque (Chq 105). The correct double-entry posting is:
options: DR Bank Account / CR Rent Account
DR Rent Account / CR Capital Account
DR Rent Account / CR Bank Account
DR Sales Account / CR Rent Account
questions.explanation: Rent is an expense — expenses increase with a debit. The bank balance decreases — assets decrease with a credit. DR Rent Account / CR Bank Account (Cash Book).

questions.question: The Purchases Ledger (Creditors Ledger) contains the individual personal accounts of all suppliers to whom the business owes money.
questions.explanation: The Purchases Ledger holds one account per supplier. Its total (sum of all supplier balances) should always equal the Creditors Control Account balance in the Nominal Ledger. This is the personal ledger for creditors.

questions.question: Flour Mills Ltd account: Opening credit balance €615 (owed to them). Murphy's Bakery pays Invoice FM23 by cheque €615. New Invoice FM24 arrives for €492 gross. What is Flour Mills Ltd's closing balance?
questions.explanation: Opening: €615 Cr (owed to supplier). Payment: €615 Dr (clears the debt, balance = €0). New invoice FM24: €492 Cr (new debt). Closing balance = €492 Cr — the business now owes Flour Mills Ltd €492.

questions.question: Which of the following accounts normally carry a DEBIT balance? Select all that apply.
options: Bank Account (positive balance)
Motor Vehicles
Rent Expense
Creditors Account
questions.explanation: Assets (bank, motor vehicles) and expenses (rent) have normal debit balances. Creditors is a liability — liabilities have normal credit balances. Remembering DEADCLIC (Debits: Expenses, Assets, Drawings; Credits: Liabilities, Income, Capital) helps.

questions.question: When totalling the Purchases Day Book and posting to the Nominal Ledger, the correct double-entry is:
options: DR Purchases + DR VAT Control (Input) / CR Creditors Control (gross total)
DR Creditors Control / CR Purchases + CR VAT Control
DR Purchases (gross total only) / CR Creditors Control (gross total)
DR Bank Account / CR Purchases Account
questions.explanation: The gross total goes to Creditors Control (CR). The net total goes to Purchases (DR) and the VAT portion goes to VAT Control as Input VAT (DR). This three-way split is the standard Purchases Day Book posting.

questions.question: A contra entry in the Cash Book occurs when cash is transferred from the petty cash tin to the bank, creating entries on both sides of the Cash Book.
questions.explanation: A contra entry appears on both sides of the same Cash Book with 'C' in the folio column. Cash deposited into the bank: DR Bank column (money in), CR Cash column (money out of cash). Both entries are in the same Cash Book.

questions.question: The Sales Ledger Control Account balance in the Nominal Ledger should equal:
options: The sum of all individual customer account balances in the Sales (Debtors) Ledger
The total of all credit sales invoices issued in the period
The bank balance minus outstanding payments
The total of all individual supplier balances
questions.explanation: The Control Account is a summary account in the Nominal Ledger that should always agree with the total of all individual accounts in the subsidiary (personal) ledger. If they don't agree, there is an error to investigate.

questions.question: A Trial Balance shows total debits of €47,320 and total credits of €46,870. What does this difference most likely indicate?
options: A complete omission of a transaction
An error of principle
A one-sided posting or a transposition error affecting only one side
A compensating error
questions.explanation: A difference of €450 (47,320 − 46,870) indicates a one-sided posting (one side is missing or wrong). A complete omission (A) and errors of principle (B) leave both sides equal. Compensating errors (D) also leave the TB balanced.

questions.question: An 'error of original entry' occurs when both sides of a transaction are recorded with the same incorrect amount (e.g., €290 entered instead of €920).
questions.explanation: An error of original entry: the wrong amount is used on both sides — debits still equal credits, so the Trial Balance remains balanced. The error is in the original value entered, not in which accounts were used or whether both sides were posted.

questions.question: A Trial Balance shows total debits of €28,450 and total credits of €27,950. A Suspense Account is opened to balance the TB. What is the balance of the Suspense Account?
questions.explanation: Difference = €28,450 − €27,950 = €500. The Suspense Account is opened with a credit balance of €500 to make total credits = €28,450, balancing the Trial Balance while the error is investigated.

questions.question: Which of the following accounts would normally appear on the CREDIT side of the Trial Balance? Select all that apply.
options: Sales Account
Capital Account
Bank Loan (long-term liability)
Purchases Account
questions.explanation: Income (Sales), capital, and liabilities (Bank Loan) have credit balances on the TB. Purchases is an expense account with a debit balance. Remember: assets and expenses on the debit side; income, liabilities, and capital on the credit side.
